0:00 / 11:06
Let’s start by returning to our description of the Validate Process
from the original TDB Fall 2020 Enrollment email series:
uring the discover phase, we de�ned a minimum viable
audience (MVA) that we seek to serve, and identi�ed 1-3
signi�cant problems (or meaningful desires) we can charge money to
help that audience solve (or ful�ll).
When we listen closely to the subtext, the nuances, the between-the-
lines of what our MVA are saying about the problems they care about
solving, ideas emerge (often “hidden” within the noise).
It’s our job to �nd these insights, then highlight and express them in
our o�ers. Validation begins when we present our ideas for solutions
directly to our minimum viable audience to assess their reactions and
gauge their level of interest.
Often, this is where our initial attempt at explaining an o�er
happens…
An o�er is the marketing expression of a product or service at a
speci�c point in time. It is not the product or service itself.
When we share a simple expression of an o�er with an MVA, the goal
is to gather data quickly that con�rms we’re on the right path, or that
Validate, Part I
MP3 File 3.81 MB
www.getwsodo.com

-- 1 of 10 --

we’ve missed the mark and need to brainstorm new ideas.
We want to know, in advance, that our ideas for solutions are likely to
produce positive results before we invest time and energy to build the
supporting infrastructure.
The last thing we want to do is create ads, websites, landing pages,
emails, and sales infrastructure for o�ers no one wants or cares
about!
Validate, Part I will focus on this part:
Validation begins when we present our ideas for solutions directly to
our minimum viable audience to assess their reactions and gauge their
level of interest.
To do that, we need to have some ideas about who is included in
our MVA (broad strokes), and we need to have a reasonably clear
understanding of the value we can offer that MVA as it relates to
the jobs they want to accomplish, pains they want to avoid, and
gains they seek to acquire.
www.getwsodo.com

-- 2 of 10 --

This is a perfect opportunity to mention that the line between
Discover and Validate is fuzzy. In fact, all of the elements of the
TDB �ywheel are more of a spectrum than a series of clearly-
delineated ‘paint-by-numbers’ steps.
Let’s consider an example from our experience with ‘Project X’
(our example business for TDB).
Initially, we researched a very broad audience of people who
expressed interest (and shared their opinions) about creativity. We
know that’s not our MVA, but we think our MVA is within that
larger audience.
As we began to compile themes, we started to notice overlap
between what we had found researching creativity in general and
another area of signi�cant interest for both of us — Personal
Knowledge Management (PKM).
www.getwsodo.com

-- 3 of 10 --

Many of the jobs, pains, and gains we had uncovered in our initial
creativity research applied perfectly to the jobs, pains, and gains
that PKM addresses, including:
▪ Producing and generating ideas.
▪ Solving problems.
▪ Making better decisions.
▪ Capturing and organizing research.
▪ Being more productive.
▪ Generating ‘ah-ha’ moments on demand.
It seems reasonable to believe that people who are interested in
PKM also are interested in creativity. However, the reverse isn’t
necessarily true (i.e., people interested in creativity are also
interested in PKM). Knowing that, we can be con�dent that we
have found a smaller audience to begin to focus our attention on.
Another factor that in�uenced our thinking is that we know the
PKM landscape reasonably well, including popular tools,
personalities, authorities, books, websites, and communities.
We’re also members of several of those communities, free and
paid, which means we can ask questions, share perspectives, and
get feedback directly from others. That will be critical for
validating our initial ideas.
We’re also our own avatar, meaning we could create something
that ‘scratches our own itch,’ knowing it probably would appeal to
others in that PKM community as well.
www.getwsodo.com

-- 4 of 10 --

Before we move on, we need to be clear that we still don’t have our
MVA clearly identi�ed. Instead, we’ve narrowed where we’re
looking for our MVA after reviewing the themes we initially
identi�ed.
We may need to repeat this process several times, �nding
audiences within audiences within audiences until we’ve found
an audience that’s small and responsive enough for us to create a
precise, attractive solution, and large enough that it could
generate enough revenue to make our work �nancially
worthwhile.
There is no magic formula to determine audience size and
�nancial viability. However, it’s worth reading (or re-reading)
Kevin Kelly’s 1,000 True Fans to better understand the nuances.
Keep in mind that the MVA you choose initially does not have to
be the audience that supports your business forever (this is an
important insight). You’re starting small to make it easier to
understand a potential audience’s needs and craft an offer that
resonates deeply with that audience.
Initially, you want the most responsive audience possible. That’s
where you’ll get the best feedback (and the highest likelihood of
making sales).
Over time, you’ll expand into new audiences as your business
grows.
Once we’ve identi�ed this smaller initial audience, the next step is
to start creating some structure for our ideas related to our
audience’s potential needs.
That’s a critical difference between Discover and Validate…
www.getwsodo.com

-- 5 of 10 --

Discover is an exercise in taking in information and looking
for patterns. It’s driven entirely by what others are saying they
want to accomplish, want to experience, or want to avoid.
Validate is when we as creators begin to take a more active
role connecting what we’ve learned about our audience with
our interests, skills, and experience.
We’ll transition from focusing on our audience’s problems to
developing, sharing, and getting feedback on our ideas for
potential solutions.
Again, we’ll use ‘Project X’ as an example.
One of the themes we identi�ed in our research was a desire to
generate ‘ah-ha’ moments on demand. We also know that
generating ideas is a very compelling reason people become
interested in personal knowledge management.
We both joined a �ve-week program in January, led by Nick Milo,
not for research speci�cally, but because we are deeply curious
learners and continuously driven to hone our learning process.
Everyone in this cohort paid $622, which is not an insigni�cant
investment. Clearly, these ‘PKM’ people are motivated to solve a
problem they have or a meaningful desire.
On the �rst call Nick asked, “why do you want to make notes?”
Below are some of the responses:
▪ manage and create knowledge
▪ to create content for my community
▪ to put my ideas in order
www.getwsodo.com

-- 6 of 10 --

▪ learn lots of things and remember things
▪ idea generation
▪ to preserve my epiphanies
▪ I want to make notes because I need to save my thoughts and
memories, track my thoughts and memories, connect my thoughts
and memories, make sense, make decisions, easily accomplish
projects on time
▪ being surprised at myself
▪ growth
▪ capture ideas and connect them to make new ideas
▪ make sense of the world
▪ improve intelligence, learning
▪ remember and refer
▪ learning and retrieval of information
▪ visualize my creativity
▪ remember things I have learned
▪ create better content
▪ developing a highly functioning ecosystem of my knowledge
▪ connections
▪ to retrieve ideas
▪ skill => collect, connect, practice reps => making sense, mastery
▪ legacy for my son
▪ to make sense of information
▪ staying agile
▪ to remember
www.getwsodo.com

-- 7 of 10 --

▪ to generate new perspectives. to make ideas more visual
▪ to increase the velocity of my projects
Some of our skills, expertise, and experience intersect perfectly
with that audience’s desire.
We’re both fascinated by the creative process, and we have studied
creativity in-depth. There’s an underlying framework for
dramatically increasing the likelihood of generating creative
insights, and we use that framework every day.
If we’re right and could teach others how to generate ‘ah-ha’
moments on demand, that seems like it would be very valuable to
the PKM audience and very likely other audiences within the
creative space.
However, we don’t yet know if that’s true. We think it could be,
and, broadly speaking, there are two ways we could �nd out.
Option #1 would be to get really excited about the idea, create a
course, build the infrastructure to sell and deliver that course, and
then start buying traf�c in hopes that our hunch was correct.
We might get lucky and be right, and that might be time well
spent. However, we might be wrong. Remember, broadly
speaking, 80% of offers fail, which means it’s probably not a great
idea to spend a lot of time and energy creating something to sell if
we’re not yet convinced anyone wants to buy it!
That leads us to option #2 — taking our ideas for a framework
that generates ‘ah-ha’ moments on-demand directly to the
personal knowledge management audience and getting their
feedback.
www.getwsodo.com

-- 8 of 10 --

We want to know if it resonates (and, if it does, how intensely).
We also want to be open to the possibility that we’re on the right
track, but our audience has even better ideas.
For example, we might hear someone say, “yes, generating ‘ah-ha’
moments on demand would be helpful, but what I really want to
accomplish is…”
Or, we might hear objections (“that’s too good to be true — no one
can generate ideas on demand…”).
We also might discover that we’re too late to the party — for
example, if someone were to say, “oh, that’s interesting … have you
read so and so’s book where she describes the process?”
There’s so much nuance to be uncovered, and keeping an open
mind is critical.
Finally, it’s entirely possible that you’ll get mixed signals. Some
people may love your idea(s), and others may not. If that happens,
look for any patterns among the two groups.
What characteristics, if any, are shared by people who respond
enthusiastically vs. those who don’t?
If you can isolate those variables, they may point the way to a
more speci�c MVA comprised of people who really engage with
your idea.
(This is another example of how we’re continuously stacking
probabilities in our favor.)
One last thought before we wrap up this module…
www.getwsodo.com

-- 9 of 10 --

Approach validation from the perspective of wide-open
curiosity. It’s really easy to fall in love with an idea and want the
world to fall in love with it too. It’s also really easy to fool yourself
with con�rmation bias, focusing on the feedback that supports
your idea and discounting everything else.
We’re all human and we can fool ourselves in predictable ways.
(We highly recommend Michael Lewis’s book, The Undoing
Project, if you’re curious about some of the many ways we
consistently fool ourselves.)
To combat these tendencies, keep an open mind and capture
whatever feedback you get. Ask questions, dig deeper, stay open to
possibilities. If you do that, you’re far more likely to identify
themes that will lead to compelling offers (and faster business
growth).
In Part II, we’ll explain how to externalize, re�ne, and distill your
ideas before you begin to share those ideas with a potential MVA
for feedback.
Part II will be a roll-up-your-sleeves “workshop” lesson. You’ll be
writing to reveal insights that will make everything else you do in
Validate easier.
Questions or feedback — please use the comments below. Use the
private accountability community to have discussions with your
peers and share your insights with them.
—André & Shawn
www.getwsodo.com

-- 10 of 10 --
